## [03:06 - 06:13]

In his book Outliers, Malcolm Gladwell writes, practice isn't the thing you do once you're good. It's the thing you do that makes you good. So do you want to have a good webinar? You probably ought to spend some time rehearsing it. Do you want to have a great webinar? I think you know what I'm going to say. So here's some suggestions from an ink article working with this same calming gal. So number one, start rehearsing early. One of the things that is pointed out in this article is that Jobs would oftentimes be months before his presentation, walking around his house, giving practices of parts of the presentation. And said he oftentimes practiced on his family during dinner. Secondly, refine every slide to make it communicate exactly what you want to the way you want to. Third, rehearse out loud like it's the real thing. So there's one thing to say it in your head. It's something totally different to say it out loud because you're not working just on the words. You're working on how it actually sounds, which brings in the tone of voice, the speed of saying it, the pauses between it, whether you slow, whether you speed up or slow down, whether you get soft or really emphasize loudly. All of those are things that are important to your presentation that don't get rehearsed unless you do it out loud. Next, ask for feedback during each step of the performance. So some of your rehearsals should be done alone, but some of it should be done with someone else listening to you and being critical, not a yes person, someone who will listen to you and critically review what you're doing. Next, schedule dress rehearsals in real world conditions. I like to actually turn on my webinar account and give it as if I was doing a webinar, making it as real world as possible. Yes, I haven't got an audience on the other end, but sometimes I actually will add an audience in, but dress rehearsals in real world conditions. And finally, in this process, keep the mood light. Don't be too hard on yourself. Don't be too hard on someone else. Let's say you've got a support person who's helping with slides and there's a typo or something like that. You know what? Keep it light. This is not the end of the world. It's webinar. It's very, very important, but keep it light. And don't be too hard on yourself in this process. Key realization, most of your peers will never rehearse their webinars.
